notes: Second AfroKitchen recipe expansion wave. Recipes are synthesized from multiple regional references and common household patterns, not copied from a single source.

recipes.name: Muamba de Galinha
recipes.description: Angolan chicken stewed with red palm oil, okra, pumpkin, tomatoes, onion, garlic, and chile until the sauce turns rich and silky.
recipes.story: Muamba de Galinha is one of Angola's best-known chicken dishes. The heart of the pot is red palm oil, okra, and chicken juices, with pumpkin or squash softening into the sauce and funge waiting beside it.
recipes.occasion: Best for a family weekend meal, celebration table, or any day when chicken stew should feel generous.
recipes.best_served_with: Funge de bombo, white rice, or boiled cassava.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use palm nut sauce instead of straight red palm oil. Pumpkin, squash, and okra amounts shift by household, but peanuts belong more naturally to muamba de ginguba than this chicken version.
recipes.review_summary: Common references agree on chicken, red palm oil or palm sauce, okra, tomato, onion, garlic, chile, and a starchy side such as funge. The AfroKitchen version keeps peanuts out of the core recipe to avoid confusing it with muamba de ginguba.
recommended_changes: Use bone-in chicken for better sauce body.
Keep the heat low after the first simmer so the palm oil emulsifies into the stew instead of breaking harshly.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Angolan ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is grounded in Angolan cuisine references and regional culinary writeups.

sources.title: Notes From a Messy Kitchen - Angola Muamba de Galinha

steps.title: Season the chicken
steps.instruction: Rub the chicken with lemon juice, garlic, salt, and pepper. Let it sit while you prep the vegetables.
steps.tip: A short rest is enough, but 1 hour in the fridge gives a fuller flavor.

steps.title: Brown and build the base
steps.instruction: Warm the palm oil in a heavy pot over medium heat. Brown the chicken in batches, then add onions and tomatoes and cook until the tomatoes soften.
steps.tip: Do not scorch the palm oil. If it smokes hard, lower the heat.

steps.title: Simmer the stew
steps.instruction: Return all chicken to the pot. Add stock, pumpkin, and the whole chile. Cover and simmer gently until the chicken is nearly tender.
steps.tip: The pumpkin should soften into the sauce without disappearing completely.

steps.title: Finish with okra
steps.instruction: Add okra and simmer uncovered until the chicken is tender and the sauce is glossy. Adjust salt and pepper before serving.
steps.tip: Add the okra late so it keeps shape and gives the sauce body.

recipes.name: Funge de Bombo
recipes.description: A smooth Angolan cassava swallow made by stirring cassava flour into hot water until thick, stretchy, and ready for stew.
recipes.story: Funge de Bombo is the Angolan cassava flour swallow that turns a stew into a meal. It is simple, but the technique matters: steady heat, strong stirring, and enough water to make it smooth rather than stiff and grainy.
recipes.occasion: Best with saucy stews, grilled fish, beans, or chicken muamba.
recipes.best_served_with: Muamba de Galinha, calulu de peixe, beans, or grilled fish.
recipes.regional_variations: Cassava flour funge is especially common in northern Angola, while cornmeal versions are also cooked in other regions.
recipes.review_summary: References agree that funge is cassava flour whisked into boiling water and stirred until thick. The AfroKitchen method starts with a slurry to reduce lumps for home cooks.
recommended_changes: Use fine cassava flour or fufu flour, not coarse toasted gari.
Keep hot water nearby so the final texture can be adjusted.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Angolan ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Angolan cuisine references and cooking guides.

ingredients.name: water
ingredients.prep_note: divided, plus hot water if needed

steps.title: Make a slurry
steps.instruction: Mix the cassava flour with 1 cup cold water until smooth and pourable.
steps.tip: The slurry method helps prevent lumps.

steps.title: Heat the water
steps.instruction: Bring the remaining 3 cups water and salt to a boil in a sturdy pot, then lower to medium heat.

steps.title: Cook and beat
steps.instruction: Pour in the slurry while stirring constantly with a wooden spoon. Keep stirring and folding until the mixture thickens, turns smooth, and pulls from the sides.
steps.tip: If it gets too stiff before it is smooth, splash in hot water and keep beating.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Wet a spoon or small bowl, shape the funge into mounds, and serve hot beside stew.

recipes.name: Calulu de Peixe
recipes.description: Angolan fish calulu with fresh fish, dried fish, okra, eggplant, tomato, greens, and palm oil layered into a deep, savory stew.
recipes.story: Calulu de Peixe is a layered Angolan fish stew where fresh fish and dried fish season the pot together. Okra, eggplant, greens, tomato, and palm oil bring the thick, coastal-meets-inland character.
recipes.occasion: Best for a slow lunch, family supper, or a table where funge needs a serious sauce.
recipes.best_served_with: Funge de bombo, rice, boiled cassava, or plantain.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use sweet potato leaves or spinach, some add zucchini or sweet potato, and the balance of fresh to dried fish changes by cook.
recipes.review_summary: Multiple sources identify fish calulu as layered fresh and dried fish with vegetables such as okra, tomato, garlic, greens, and palm oil. The AfroKitchen method keeps the layering logic and uses accessible spinach when sweet potato leaves are unavailable.
recommended_changes: Soak dried fish before cooking so the salt level is controlled.
Layer rather than aggressively stirring at the start, so the fish holds together.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Angolan ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on regional culinary references and Angolan dish references.

sources.title: Travel Food Atlas - Calulu de Peixe Angola Recipe
World Food and Wine - Calulu de Peixe

steps.title: Soak the dried fish
steps.instruction: Soak the dried fish in warm water, then rinse and break into large pieces.
steps.tip: Taste a small piece after soaking. If it is still very salty, rinse again.

steps.title: Layer the pot
steps.instruction: In a heavy pot, layer onions, tomatoes, garlic, eggplant, dried fish, fresh fish, greens, okra, palm oil, chile, and water.

steps.title: Steam and simmer
steps.instruction: Cover and cook over medium-low heat until the vegetables release liquid and the fish is cooked through.
steps.tip: Shake the pot gently instead of stirring hard.

steps.title: Thicken the sauce
steps.instruction: Uncover and simmer until the okra and eggplant thicken the sauce. Adjust seasoning and serve hot.

recipes.name: Ta'ameya
recipes.description: Egyptian fava bean falafel made with soaked split fava beans, fresh herbs, garlic, spices, and a sesame crust before frying.
recipes.story: Ta'ameya is Egypt's green, herb-heavy answer to falafel, commonly made from dried split fava beans rather than chickpeas. The best patties are crisp outside, fluffy inside, and bright with parsley, cilantro, dill, garlic, cumin, and coriander.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, mezze, street-food-style snacks, or a vegetarian sandwich spread.
recipes.best_served_with: Baladi bread, tahini sauce, pickles, chopped salad, and ful medames.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add leek or scallion, some coat heavily with sesame, and Alexandria-style versions may hide a spicy chile filling.
recipes.review_summary: Sources consistently identify Egyptian ta'ameya as fava-based, herb-rich, and fried. The AfroKitchen version uses soaked uncooked split favas, not canned beans, for proper texture.
recommended_changes: Do not boil the beans before grinding.
Chill the mixture before frying if it feels loose.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Egyptian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is grounded in Egyptian culinary references and recipe sources.

sources.title: The Egyptian Cook - Traditional Egyptian Breakfast Ful wa Ta'ameya
Polkadot Passport - Egyptian Fava Bean Falafel

steps.title: Soak the beans
steps.instruction: Soak split fava beans overnight in plenty of water, then drain very well before grinding.
steps.tip: Dry beans are essential for texture. Canned beans will make the batter collapse.

steps.title: Grind the batter
steps.instruction: Pulse fava beans, herbs, scallions, garlic, cumin, coriander, salt, and pepper until finely ground but not wet or pureed.

steps.title: Season and shape
steps.instruction: Mix in baking powder. Shape small patties and press sesame seeds onto one side.

steps.title: Fry until crisp
steps.instruction: Fry in hot oil until deep golden and crisp, turning once. Drain and serve hot.
steps.tip: Fry a test patty first and adjust salt before shaping the rest.

recipes.name: Tibs
recipes.description: Ethiopian-style sauteed beef with onion, garlic, rosemary, berbere, niter kibbeh, and optional awaze for heat.
recipes.story: Tibs sits between a quick saute and a feast dish: hot pan, tender meat, onion, chile, rosemary, niter kibbeh, and berbere or awaze depending on the cook. It should taste bold but still let the browned meat lead.
recipes.occasion: Best for a fast celebratory supper, injera platter, or special lunch.
recipes.best_served_with: Injera, awaze, salad, shiro, or mild vegetable sides.
recipes.regional_variations: Some tibs are mild and buttery, some are spicy with berbere or awaze, and some are served sizzling with peppers and onions.
recipes.review_summary: References agree on small pieces of meat cooked quickly with onion and Ethiopian seasonings. The AfroKitchen version keeps berbere optional in amount because some tibs styles use awaze on the side instead of a heavily red sauce.
recommended_changes: Brown the beef in batches so it sears instead of steaming.
Use niter kibbeh if available, but ghee with a pinch of cardamom and cumin is a workable home substitute.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Ethiopian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Ethiopian culinary references and regional recipe sources.

sources.title: Rimping - Beef Tibs Ethiopian Spiced Beef

ingredients.name: niter kibbeh
ingredients.substitution: ghee with a pinch of cumin and cardamom

steps.title: Season the meat
steps.instruction: Pat the meat dry and season with salt, pepper, and half the berbere.
steps.tip: Dry meat browns faster and stays juicier.

steps.title: Sear hot
steps.instruction: Heat half the niter kibbeh in a wide pan. Sear the meat in batches until browned outside but not fully cooked through.

steps.title: Cook the aromatics
steps.instruction: Add remaining niter kibbeh, onion, garlic, ginger, chile, rosemary, and tomato if using. Cook until the onion softens.

steps.title: Finish the tibs
steps.instruction: Return the meat to the pan with remaining berbere and optional awaze. Toss over high heat until glossy and just cooked.
steps.tip: Serve immediately so the meat stays tender.

recipes.name: Chicken Pastilla
recipes.description: A Moroccan savory-sweet pie of spiced chicken, soft egg sauce, toasted almonds, cinnamon, and crisp pastry.
recipes.story: Chicken Pastilla is Morocco's celebration pie: savory chicken cooked with onion and warm spices, eggs stirred into the reduced sauce, almonds sweetened with cinnamon, all wrapped in thin pastry and dusted lightly at the end.
recipes.occasion: Best for Eid tables, weddings, special dinners, or a showpiece weekend meal.
recipes.best_served_with: A simple salad, mint tea, olives, or a light soup.
recipes.regional_variations: Traditional versions may use pigeon and warqa pastry. Home versions often use chicken and phyllo for access.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on the layered savory-sweet form: chicken, eggs, almonds, cinnamon, sugar, and thin pastry. The AfroKitchen version uses phyllo as an accessible substitute for warqa.
recommended_changes: Reduce the chicken sauce before adding eggs so the filling is not wet.
Let the filling cool before assembling to keep the pastry crisp.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Moroccan ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Moroccan culinary references and recipe writeups.

sources.title: Asia Society Korea - Moroccan Chicken Pastilla with Almonds

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.instruction: Simmer chicken with onion, butter, ginger, cinnamon, turmeric, herbs, salt, pepper, and a little water until tender.

steps.title: Make the egg sauce
steps.instruction: Remove and shred the chicken. Reduce the onion sauce until thick, then stir in beaten eggs over low heat until softly set.
steps.tip: The filling should be moist, not runny.

steps.title: Prepare almonds
steps.instruction: Mix toasted chopped almonds with powdered sugar and cinnamon.

steps.title: Layer and bake
steps.instruction: Brush phyllo sheets with butter in a round pan. Layer chicken, egg sauce, and almonds, fold pastry over, brush again, and bake until golden.
steps.tip: Cool 10 minutes before dusting with a little sugar and cinnamon.

recipes.name: Poulet DG
recipes.description: Cameroonian chicken with fried plantains, carrots, green beans, peppers, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, and herbs.
recipes.story: Poulet DG, often read as 'director general chicken', is Cameroonian abundance in one pan: chicken, ripe plantain, and vegetables cooked glossy and generous enough for guests.
recipes.occasion: Best for parties, Sunday lunch, and any table that wants one colorful centerpiece.
recipes.best_served_with: Serve as a complete plate or with rice for a larger meal.
recipes.regional_variations: Vegetable choices vary, with carrots, green beans, peppers, celery, leeks, and tomatoes all common. Plantains are usually fried before joining the sauce.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on chicken, fried plantains, and a colorful vegetable sauce. The AfroKitchen version keeps the plantains separate until the end so they stay sweet-edged rather than mushy.
recommended_changes: Use ripe but firm plantains so they fry cleanly.
Add fried plantains near the end to preserve their texture.
recipes.official_source_status: A Cameroon embassy travel-advice PDF references Poulet DG as a featured dish, but no official ministry-standard recipe was found in this pass. The recipe is based on Cameroonian culinary references.

sources.title: Embassy of Cameroon in Japan - Travel Advices PDF

steps.title: Season and brown
steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt, pepper, garlic, and ginger. Brown in a wide pot until golden on several sides.

steps.title: Fry the plantains
steps.instruction: Fry plantain slices in hot oil until golden on both sides. Drain and set aside.
steps.tip: Firm ripe plantains hold their shape best.

steps.title: Make the vegetable sauce
steps.instruction: Cook onions, tomatoes, carrots, green beans, and peppers with the chicken until the sauce thickens and the chicken is cooked through.

steps.title: Fold together
steps.instruction: Add fried plantains and herbs. Toss gently until glossy and hot, then serve.

recipes.name: Kontomire Stew
recipes.description: Ghanaian cocoyam leaf stew with palm oil, egusi, smoked fish, tomatoes, onion, chile, and tender greens.
recipes.story: Kontomire stew is one of Ghana's beloved green stews, often cooked with cocoyam leaves, palm oil, egusi, smoked fish, and a tomato-pepper base. It is earthy, rich, and built for yam, plantain, rice, or ampesi.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekday family meals, yam and plantain plates, or a hearty lunch.
recipes.best_served_with: Boiled yam, ripe plantain, rice, ampesi, or gari.
recipes.regional_variations: Spinach can stand in for cocoyam leaves when needed. Some cooks use salted fish, eggs, snails, beef, or no animal protein at all.
recipes.review_summary: References consistently identify kontomire as cocoyam leaves cooked into stew with palm oil, tomato, onion, pepper, egusi, and smoked or salted fish. The AfroKitchen version preserves those anchors and allows spinach as an access substitute.
recommended_changes: Soak or rinse salted fish before adding.
Stir greens gently so the fish and egusi curds do not break completely.
recipes.official_source_status: A Ghana food research commentary supports the common composition, but no single official recipe standard was found in this pass.

sources.title: Fafa Gilbert - Kontomire Stew Cocoyam Leaves
African Journal of Food Agriculture Nutrition and Development commentary

ingredients.name: egusi or agushi seeds
ingredients.prep_note: ground and mixed with a little water

steps.title: Prep the greens
steps.instruction: Rinse and chop the kontomire leaves. If using mature leaves, blanch briefly and drain.

steps.title: Cook the tomato base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil and cook onions until soft. Add tomato, chile, ginger, and garlic, then simmer until the sauce thickens.

steps.title: Set the egusi
steps.instruction: Spoon in the egusi paste and let it steam for a few minutes before gently stirring into curds.
steps.tip: Letting egusi set first gives the stew body.

steps.title: Finish the stew
steps.instruction: Add smoked fish and greens. Cover and cook until tender, then adjust salt and serve.

recipes.name: Binyebwa
recipes.description: Ugandan groundnut sauce made from peanut paste simmered with onion, tomato, and gentle seasoning until creamy.
recipes.story: Binyebwa is Uganda's beloved groundnut sauce, simple but deeply comforting. Peanut paste cooks slowly with onion, tomato, and water until the oil rises and the sauce becomes smooth enough for matoke, sweet potatoes, rice, or greens.
recipes.occasion: Best for everyday meals, vegetarian plates, and matoke lunches.
recipes.best_served_with: Matoke, sweet potatoes, cassava, rice, greens, or millet bread.
recipes.regional_variations: Some households add smoked fish, meat, mushrooms, or greens. This base version stays vegetarian so it can sit beside many Ugandan plates.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree that binyebwa is a Ugandan groundnut sauce built from ground peanuts or peanut paste, onion, tomato, and slow simmering until the sauce separates slightly and thickens.
recommended_changes: Use unsweetened peanut paste or freshly ground roasted peanuts.
Simmer gently and stir often, because peanut sauces catch easily.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Ugandan ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Ugandan culinary references and aid/food education recipe sheets.

sources.title: World Vision Australia - Binyebwa Recipe PDF

ingredients.name: unsweetened peanut paste or ground roasted peanuts

steps.title: Loosen the paste
steps.instruction: Whisk peanut paste with warm water until smooth and pourable.

steps.title: Cook the aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pot and cook onion until soft. Add tomato and simmer until the raw taste fades.

steps.title: Simmer slowly
steps.instruction: Add the peanut mixture, pepper, and salt. Simmer on low heat, stirring often, until thick and creamy.
steps.tip: When a little peanut oil rises to the top, the sauce is ready.

steps.title: Adjust and serve
steps.instruction: Thin with hot water if needed, add optional mushrooms or greens until tender, and serve warm.

recipes.name: Somali Sambusa
recipes.description: Crisp Somali fried pastries filled with spiced beef, onion, garlic, cilantro, chile, and xawaash.
recipes.story: Sambusa is a Ramadan favorite and party snack across Somali homes. The triangle is crisp, the filling is dry enough to keep the pastry flaky, and xawaash gives the beef its warm Somali signature.
recipes.occasion: Best for Ramadan iftar, tea time, parties, and snack trays.
recipes.best_served_with: Green chutney, basbaas, lemon wedges, or Somali tea.
recipes.regional_variations: Fillings can be beef, lamb, chicken, tuna, or vegetables. Homemade wrappers are common, but spring roll wrappers are a practical shortcut.
recipes.review_summary: Sources support Somali sambusa as fried pastry filled with ground meat or vegetables and Somali spices. The AfroKitchen version uses a dry beef filling with xawaash and cilantro so the wrappers stay crisp.
recommended_changes: Cook the filling until dry before wrapping.
Seal triangles with flour paste so they do not open in the oil.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Somali ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Somali culinary references and community recipe sheets.

steps.title: Cook the filling
steps.instruction: Cook beef with onion, garlic, chile, xawaash, cumin, salt, and pepper until browned and dry. Stir in cilantro and cool.
steps.tip: A wet filling makes the wrappers split.

steps.title: Fold triangles
steps.instruction: Place filling on each wrapper strip and fold into tight triangles. Seal the edge with flour paste.

steps.title: Fry in batches
steps.instruction: Fry sambusas in hot oil until golden and crisp on both sides.

steps.title: Drain and serve
steps.instruction: Drain on a rack or paper towel and serve hot with basbaas or lemon.

recipes.name: Milk Tart
recipes.description: South African milk tart with a sweet pastry shell, creamy milk custard, and cinnamon on top.
recipes.story: Melktert is the South African milk custard tart that tastes like a quiet afternoon: sweet crust, soft milk filling, and cinnamon on top. It is lighter and milkier than many egg custard pies.
recipes.occasion: Best for tea time, Sunday dessert, birthdays, and make-ahead entertaining.
recipes.best_served_with: Rooibos tea, coffee, or fresh fruit.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions bake the custard in the shell, while others cook the custard first and set it in a blind-baked crust.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on a sweet pastry crust filled with milk custard made from milk, sugar, eggs, flour or starch, and cinnamon. The AfroKitchen version uses the cooked-custard method for reliable texture.
recommended_changes: Infuse the milk with cinnamon for a deeper flavor.
Chill the tart fully before slicing.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct South African tourism or ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on South African culinary references.

sources.title: The South African - Perfect South African Milk Tart

steps.title: Infuse the milk
steps.instruction: Heat milk with the cinnamon stick until steaming. Turn off heat and let it infuse briefly, then remove the stick.

steps.title: Mix the custard
steps.instruction: Whisk sugar, eggs, cornstarch, flour, and salt until smooth. Slowly whisk in hot milk.

steps.title: Cook until thick
steps.instruction: Return custard to the pot and cook over medium-low heat, whisking constantly, until thick and glossy. Stir in butter and vanilla.
steps.tip: Whisk the corners of the pot so no starch clumps hide there.

steps.title: Fill and chill
steps.instruction: Pour custard into the pastry shell, dust with cinnamon, and chill until set before slicing.

recipes.name: Akara
recipes.description: Nigerian black-eyed pea fritters blended with onion and pepper, whipped light, then fried until crisp outside and fluffy inside.
recipes.story: Akara is one of Nigeria's great breakfast and street foods: peeled black-eyed peas, onion, pepper, salt, and hot oil. The magic is in peeling well and whipping air into the batter before frying.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, Saturday street-food plates, Ramadan iftar, or a snack tray.
recipes.best_served_with: Pap, custard, agege bread, ogi, hot sauce, or garri.
recipes.regional_variations: Across West Africa, related bean fritters appear as akara, koose, akla, and more. Nigerian versions often stay simple with onion and chile, while richer versions may add shrimp or bell pepper.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on peeled black-eyed peas blended with pepper and onion, then deep fried. The AfroKitchen version is Nigerian-focused and separate from the existing Sierra Leone banana akara route.
recommended_changes: Use peeled beans for the classic light texture.
Blend with very little water so the batter can hold shape in hot oil.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian and West African culinary references.

sources.title: My Diaspora Kitchen - Akara Balls Black Eyed Peas Fritters

steps.title: Soak and peel
steps.instruction: Soak beans until softened, rub off the skins, and rinse until mostly clean.
steps.tip: Peeling is the work, but it gives akara its light texture.

steps.title: Blend thick
steps.instruction: Blend peeled beans with onion, peppers, and as little water as possible until smooth and thick.

steps.title: Whip the batter
steps.instruction: Beat the batter with salt until slightly lighter and airy.
steps.tip: Air in the batter helps the fritters puff.

steps.title: Fry the akara
steps.instruction: Drop spoonfuls into hot oil and fry until deep golden, turning once. Drain and serve hot.
